data.title: A Look At Star Plus Legend Holdings’ Valuation As Guidance Swings From Profit To Loss

Guidance points to profit to loss swing

Star Plus Legend Holdings (SEHK:6683) has guided for a consolidated loss of up to CN¥52.0 million for 2025, compared with a consolidated profit of CN¥50.2 million for 2024.

The board links this shift mainly to slower IP content launches affecting its IP creation segment, lower gross margins in the expanding new retail business, and a non cash impairment provision on certain trade receivables tied to recent geopolitical developments.

See our latest analysis for Star Plus Legend Holdings.

Despite the profit to loss guidance, Star Plus Legend Holdings’ 1 day share price return of 16.86% pushed the stock to HK$8.25. Preferred price to sales of 10.3x, is it justified?

On a P/S of 10.3x at a last close of HK$8.25, Star Plus Legend Holdings trades at a much richer revenue multiple than many peers in its sector.

The P/S ratio compares the company’s market value with its revenue. A 10.3x reading means investors are currently paying HK$10.30 in market value for every HK$1 of sales. For a business focused on health products, celebrity IP and retail, this kind of premium often reflects expectations around future scaling of revenue or earnings quality that goes beyond the latest reported figures.

That premium looks particularly stretched when you compare it with the Hong Kong Food industry average P/S of 0.8x and a peer average of 1.1x, both far below 10.3x. data.title: Butler National (OTCPK:BUKS) Margin Expansion Reinforces Bullish Earnings Narratives In Q3 2026
data.description:
Butler National (BUKS) has reported Q3 2026 revenue of US$26.9 million and basic EPS of US$0.10, set against trailing twelve month revenue of US$91.9 million and EPS of US$0.30 that came with 44.1% earnings growth and a 21.4% net margin over the past year. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$21.2 million in Q3 2025 to US$26.9 million in Q3 2026 and basic EPS from US$0.05 to US$0.10 across the same periods, with trailing net income reaching US$19.7 million, which positions the latest release as part of a period where profitability and margins are central to how investors read the story.

44.1% TTM earnings growth behind the headline

  • Over the last 12 months, Butler National generated US$19.7 million of net income and Basic EPS of US$0.30 on US$91.9 million of revenue, with earnings reported as growing 44.1% year over year.
  • What is striking for a bullish view is how the 44.1% earnings growth lines up with a 29.9% five year annual earnings growth rate. This suggests recent performance is stronger than the longer term trend:
    • Supporters pointing to consistency over time can reference that the company has been profitable for at least five years, with that 29.9% annual growth rate backing a longer track record rather than a one off year.
    • At the same time, anyone leaning on a bullish angle still needs to keep in mind that this acceleration is judged only on trailing figures. The key question is whether the current US$6.7 million Q3 net income fits into a steady pattern or a particularly strong run.

Margins sit at 21.4% on a trailing basis

  • Trailing net margin is reported at 21.4%, compared with 16.1% the prior year, while Q3 2026 net income of US$6.7 million on US$26.9 million of revenue gives a similar picture of profitability on recent sales.
  • Bears who question the durability of higher profitability have to weigh that concern against the margin profile now on record:
    • The step from a 16.1% net margin last year to 21.4% on a trailing basis sits alongside quarterly net income rising from US$3.3 million in Q4 2025 to US$6.7 million in Q3 2026. Recent quarters therefore echo the stronger profitability seen in the trailing figures.
    • On the other hand, skeptics can fairly point out that margins are being viewed only over a one year comparison window, so they may want to see how future quarters compare to the current 21.4% level before calling it a settled trend.

P/E of 12.1x against richer industry multiples

  • At a share price of US$3.70 and trailing Basic EPS of US$0.30, Butler National trades on a P/E of 12.1x, which sits well below both the reported peer average of 35.7x and the US Aerospace & Defense industry average of 42.3x. The DCF fair value is cited at US$5.66 per share versus the current market price.
  • What stands out for investors weighing a balanced narrative is the gap between these valuation measures and the earnings profile:
    • On one side, the DCF fair value of US$5.66 compared to a US$3.70 share price, along with a 21.4% net margin and 44.1% trailing earnings growth, gives quantitative support for investors who view the current multiple as conservative relative to both fundamentals and the higher peer and industry P/E levels.
    • On the other side, the fact that the market is still assigning a 12.1x P/E despite those metrics leaves room for the view that investors may be pricing in company specific risks or simply applying a discount compared with the wider Aerospace & Defense group.

data.title: Has Mohawk Industries (MHK) Become More Attractive After Recent 24% Share Price Slide

  • The stock has seen a 24.0% decline over the last 30 days and is down 10.3% over the past year, even though the 3 year return sits at 8.7%.

This context has kept attention on the stock as investors reassess the balance between cyclicality, cost pressures and long term demand for its products.

Find out why Mohawk Industries's -10.3% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Mohawk Industries Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to today’s dollars to estimate what the whole business could be worth right now.

For Mohawk Industries, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The company’s last twelve month free cash flow is about $559.9 million. Analyst inputs and Simply Wall St extrapolations point to free cash flow of $712.1 million in 2035, with intermediate years such as 2026 and 2028 projected at $601.5 million and $655.0 million respectively, all in USD and all below $1 billion.

When those projected cash flows are discounted back, the DCF model suggests an estimated intrinsic value of about $151.83 per share, compared with the recent share price of roughly $102.55. That implies the stock is trading at a 32.5% discount to this DCF estimate, indicating potential undervaluation on this model alone.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Over recent periods the company has seen quarterly revenue move from €182.5 million in Q4 2024 to €437.1 million in Q4 2025, while quarterly EPS shifted from €0.34 to €0.77. This sets up a results season where investors are likely to focus on how sustainable these earnings are against softer net profit margins.

See our full analysis for Grenke.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the prevailing narratives around Grenke's growth potential, risk profile, and profitability trajectory.

See what the community is saying about Grenke

Advertisement

Margins Under Pressure At 7.5%

  • Over the last 12 months, Grenke earned €69.8 million on €924.3 million of revenue, which works out to a 7.5% net profit margin compared with 11.3% in the prior year.
  • Consensus narrative supporters point out that this lower margin sits alongside digitalisation efforts and new-market expansion, yet:
    • Trailing earnings still rose 6.6% over the year, so profitability and margin trends are telling slightly different stories.
    • Ongoing restructuring and higher risk costs are cited as headwinds, and the current 7.5% margin illustrates how those costs are flowing through to the bottom line.

Quarterly Profit Swing Across 2025

  • Within FY 2025, net income moved from a small loss of €0.7 million in Q1 to €33.9 million in Q4, while quarterly revenue ranged from €155.0 million to €437.1 million and EPS went from a slight loss to €0.77.
  • Bulls argue that cost discipline and digitalisation can support higher long term margins, and this pattern in 2025 interacts with that story in a few ways:
    • The move from a loss in Q1 to positive net income and EPS in later quarters fits with the bullish view that profitability can improve as efficiency gains build.
    • At the same time, the wide spread between early year results and Q4 highlights how sensitive earnings still are to costs and credit expenses, which bulls need to keep in mind.

Bulls say Grenke's sharp profit recovery through 2025 could be the early sign of the earnings ramp they expect. However, the step up from a Q1 loss to a strong Q4 also shows how dependent that story is on keeping costs and risk charges in check. 🐂 Grenke Bull Case

Cheap P/E, But Cash Flow Strain

  • With the share price at €13.26, Grenke trades on a P/E of 8.4x and about 34.5% below a DCF fair value of €20.24, while net profit margins are 7.5% and debt and the 3.02% dividend are not well covered by operating and free cash flows.
  • Bears focus on these weaker cash flow signals and margin compression, and the current numbers give them some support:
    • The gap between the 7.5% margin and last year’s 11.3% margin aligns with concerns that higher risk costs and expenses are eating into profitability.
    • The fact that both debt and the dividend are not well covered by cash flows shows why some investors worry that a low P/E and discount to DCF fair value might reflect real balance sheet and payout constraints.

Skeptics point out that a low 8.4x P/E and discount to DCF fair value mean less if cash flows are struggling to support debt and dividends. This is exactly the pressure they see in Grenke’s latest numbers. 🐻 Grenke Bear Case

Next Steps

If this mix of strengths and pressure points feels finely balanced, take a moment now to weigh the numbers yourself and shape your own view, then check out 4 key rewards and 3 important warning signs to see how those potential risks and rewards line up.

Explore Alternatives

Why ASA Gold and Precious Metals (ASA) is back on growth investors’ radar

ASA Gold and Precious Metals (ASA) has drawn fresh attention after being flagged as a potential CANSLIM growth name, supported by strong quarterly and annual earnings metrics, as well as a balance sheet with no debt.

See our latest analysis for ASA Gold and Precious Metals.

Even after a recent 7 day share price return of around an 11% decline and a 30 day share price return of about a 10% decline from the latest close of US$65.16, the 1 year total shareholder return of about 143% and 3 year total shareholder return of over 3x suggest momentum has been strong rather than fading.

If ASA’s move has you rethinking exposure to precious metals, it could be a good moment to widen the net with our 28 elite gold producer stocks and see what else is standing out.

With ASA trading at an implied premium to some intrinsic estimates, but backed by strong recent returns and no debt, you have to ask: is there still a buying opportunity here, or is the market already pricing in future growth?

Price-to-earnings of 2x: Is it justified?

On a simple P/E lens, ASA Gold and Precious Metals looks cheap, with a 2x P/E compared with both the broader US market and its own capital markets peers.

The P/E ratio compares the current share price to earnings per share, so a lower multiple can suggest the market is paying less for each dollar of current earnings. For an investment company focused on precious metals, that kind of gap can hint that investors are cautious about how stable those earnings are, or how repeatable they might be.

Here, the context matters. ASA has very strong historic earnings growth, including a very large one off gain of $667.1m affecting the last 12 months to 30 November 2025. Its earnings growth over the past year is described as very large relative to both its own 5 year history and the US Capital Markets industry. That kind of one off boost can pull the P/E down sharply, making the headline 2x multiple look more attractive than it might be if earnings normalise.

Even so, the comparison is stark. ASA's 2x P/E is far below the US Capital Markets industry average of 22.8x, and also well under its peer average of 8x. That is a wide gap, and if earnings were to settle at levels closer to the recent figures, the market would need to move a long way before ASA traded on similar terms to its sector and peers.

See what the numbers say about this price — find out in our valuation breakdown.

data.title: Has Envista Holdings (NVST) Pulled Back Too Far After Recent Share Price Weakness?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Envista Holdings is attractively priced right now, this article will walk through what the current share price might be implying about its value.
  • The stock last closed at US$25.44, with a 56.6% return over the past year, a 17.3% gain year to date, but declines of 8.9% over 7 days and 15.7% over 30 days that may have shifted how investors think about its risk and reward.
  • Recent news flow around Envista has focused on its position in the healthcare space and how investors interpret that alongside longer term returns, including a 33.7% decline over 3 years and a 37.4% decline over 5 years. Together with the strong 1 year and year to date figures, this context helps frame whether the current price feels stretched or still leaves room based on fundamentals.
  • Envista currently scores 1 out of 6 on our valuation checks, indicating it screens as undervalued on just one metric. You can see the breakdown in our valuation score of 1/6. Next we will look at how different valuation approaches line up on Envista, and then finish with a more holistic way to think about what the market might be pricing in.

Envista Holdings scores just 1/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Envista Holdings Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today, using a required return that reflects risk.

For Envista Holdings, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $227.8 million. Analysts and internal estimates project free cash flow rising to $309 million by 2029, with a series of detailed annual projections between 2026 and 2035 that are then discounted to reflect the time value of money and risk.

Putting these cash flow projections together with the discount rate produces an estimated intrinsic value of about $29.86 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $25.44, this implies the stock trades at roughly a 14.8% discount to the model’s estimate, which screens as undervalued under this approach.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Envista Holdings is undervalued by 14.8%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 47 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Envista Holdings.

Approach 2: Envista Holdings Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Envista Holdings, the P/E ratio is a useful way to gauge what investors are currently willing to pay for each dollar of earnings. Higher growth expectations and lower perceived risk generally support a higher P/E, while slower growth and higher risk usually line up with a lower, more conservative P/E range.

Envista currently trades on a P/E of 88.70x. That sits well above the Medical Equipment industry average of 26.87x and also above the peer group average of 48.94x. To add more context, Simply Wall St also looks at a proprietary “Fair Ratio”, which is the P/E it would expect for Envista given factors such as its earnings profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and specific risks.

For Envista, this Fair Ratio is 32.14x. This can be more informative than a simple comparison with peers or the broad industry because it adjusts for the company’s own characteristics rather than assuming all Medical Equipment stocks deserve similar multiples. Compared with the current P/E of 88.70x, Envista’s share price screens as expensive on this approach.

Result: OVERVALUED

P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 18 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Envista Holdings Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. On Simply Wall St’s Community page you can use Narratives, where you tell a clear story about Envista Holdings, link that story to specific assumptions for future revenue, earnings and margins, and see the fair value that results. You can then compare this with the current price to help decide what action, if any, makes sense for you. Those Narratives update automatically as new news or earnings arrive and can accommodate very different views, such as a more bearish fair value around US$17.00 or a more optimistic fair value around US$35.00 based on the widest analyst range for Envista today.

For Envista Holdings however, we'll make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Envista Holdings Narratives:

🐂 Envista Holdings Bull Case

Fair value in this bullish narrative: US$28.46 per share

Implied discount to that fair value from the last close of US$25.44: about 10.6%

Revenue growth assumption: 3.91%

  • Analysts in this camp lean on growth in emerging markets and digital products, supported by higher R&D spend and new AI driven dentistry offerings.
  • They factor in margin improvement from cost cuts, operational efficiencies and closer ties with Dental Service Organizations that can support more recurring revenue.
  • The updated fair value of US$28.46 reflects higher assumed future P/E multiples and slightly higher revenue growth, balanced against more conservative profit margin assumptions.

🐻 Envista Holdings Bear Case

Fair value in this bearish narrative: US$20.03 per share

Implied premium to that fair value from the last close of US$25.44: about 27.0%

Revenue growth assumption: 3.72%

  • This view leans on the idea that higher valuation multiples could be hard to support if profitability does not keep pace with expectations.
  • Analysts here point to execution risk around improving margins and delivering on earnings targets, even though revenue growth and a higher future P/E multiple are built into the fair value.
  • The revised fair value of about US$20.03 includes slightly higher assumed revenue growth and future P/E, but with lower net margin assumptions that temper the upside case.

Put simply, you have a bullish narrative that sees today's price sitting below its fair value estimate, and a bearish narrative that views the same price as rich. The useful exercise is to compare their assumptions with your own view of Envista's growth, margins and risk, then decide which story, if either, feels closer to how you see the stock.
